title_of_original: Judgment of the Court of Justice of 17 July 2008, Essent Netwerk Noord, C-206/06
decided_by: Court of Justice of the European Union
case_description: [1] Portugal operated a state-coordinated, vertically integrated electricity system. [2] Electricity generation was carried out by four regional public undertakings, [3] which jointly owned LUSELEC SA, a central entity responsible for overseeing national electricity production, long-term supply security, and energy imports. [4] LUSELEC's mandate, conferred through government resolution, was to ensure reliable and affordable electricity supply in accordance with national planning priorities and energy policy objectives. [5] To fulfil its role, LUSELEC engaged in a variety of long-term investment projects and contractual undertakings. [6] Some of these were directly encouraged or required by the Portuguese State. [7] For example, LUSELEC was instructed to build a high-capacity back-up gas turbine facility in the interior region to ensure supply stability during summer peaks, [8] even though such demand rarely materialised. [9] The government also required the financing of rural grid extensions under the Universal Access Programme, [10] despite the lack of short-term profitability. [11] In addition to these policy-driven projects, [12] LUSELEC entered into several long-term electricity import contracts with foreign suppliers on its own initiative [13] and funded speculative R&D ventures in biomass gasification, which ultimately failed to yield marketable technology. [14] At the time, Portugal's electricity sector operated under a framework that allowed costs to be pooled and spread across distribution companies. [15] These costs were then passed on to end users through tariffs that were set or approved by the Ministry of Energy and Infrastructure. [16] In 2006, Portugal initiated a comprehensive liberalisation of its electricity market. [17] The reform introduced structural separation between generation, transmission, and retail supply, and opened the sector to competition from independent producers. [18] To implement these changes, the Portuguese legislature adopted the Electricity Sector Reform Act in 2007, [19] which entered into force on 1 January 2008. [20] The new law brought an end to LUSELEC’s role as central coordinator and replaced regulated pricing with a system based on market-determined rates. [21] Liberalisation left behind a series of stranded costs. [22] These were financial obligations undertaken under the prior regulatory regime that could no longer be recovered in a competitive market. [23] The burden of these stranded costs fell primarily on the generating undertakings, [24] which had collectively funded the investments through their joint control of LUSELEC. [25] The stranded costs were considerable and covered a range of activities, not all of which were equally justified. [26] To determine which stranded costs should be compensated, the government established a technical committee chaired by Professor Helena Santos. [27] The Santos Committee delivered its report in late 2007. It recommended that certain costs be treated as eligible for public compensation, specifically those tied to government-imposed obligations. [28] These included the interior-region gas turbine facility and the Universal Access Programme. [29] In contrast, the committee found that costs related to speculative R&D and the long-term import contracts had been undertaken by LUSELEC as part of its own commercial strategy [30] and should be borne by the undertakings themselves. [31] Prior to the full entry into force of liberalisation, [32] the government had facilitated a voluntary arrangement known as the Sectoral Adjustment Agreement. [33] In place from 2004 to 2007, this agreement involved payments from distribution companies to LUSELEC totalling EUR 423.84 million annually to help cover stranded costs incurred during the transition. [34] The agreement did not distinguish between costs arising from State-imposed obligations and those resulting from LUSELEC’s own commercial decisions. [35] It treated the stranded costs as a unified category and required distribution companies to contribute accordingly. [36] Although the arrangement was not based in legislation, it functioned as a comprehensive mechanism for sectoral cost-sharing. [37] The agreement expired on 31 December 2007 and could not be renewed under the new liberalised regime. [38] As a result, there was no legal mechanism in place to secure the final payment for the costs incurred in the second half of 2007. [39] To address this problem, the Portuguese legislature enacted Article 12 of the Transitional Energy Measures Act on 28 December 2007, with retroactive effect to 1 July 2007. [40] Article 12 introduced a statutory surcharge of EUR 0.0000678 per kilowatt hour on all electricity transmitted to final customers between 1 July and 31 December 2007, excluding protected users. [41] The surcharge was expressly intended to replicate the financial contribution that would have been made under the final year of the Sectoral Adjustment Agreement. [42] Like the agreement it replaced, Article 12 applied to the entire pool of stranded costs incurred in 2007, [43] without distinguishing between costs linked to public policy obligations and those arising from LUSELEC’s autonomous commercial activities. [44] Under Article 12, licensed network operators and suppliers were required to collect the surcharge from customers as part of their standard billing and to transfer the funds to LUSELEC, [45] which had been designated as the recipient entity. [46] The transfers were to be completed by 30 June 2008. [47] The total amount collectable under this scheme was capped at EUR 423.84 million. [48] If the proceeds exceeded that amount, LUSELEC was required to remit the surplus to the Ministry of Energy and Infrastructure, [49] which would allocate it for future public infrastructure subsidies identified in the Duarte Committee’s findings. [50] Importantly, LUSELEC was not authorised to use the collected surcharge for any purpose other than those explicitly outlined in the law. [51] Article 12 required LUSELEC to maintain detailed accounts of all receipts and expenditures, [52] and these records had to be audited by an independent auditor in accordance with Portuguese commercial law. [53] This accountability framework was designed to ensure that the funds served strictly public-interest functions [54] and were not diverted for discretionary use by LUSELEC or its shareholders.
